questions.question: The 2^n vertices of a graph G corresponds to all subsets of a set of size n, for n >= 6. Two vertices of G are adjacent if and only if the corresponding sets intersect in exactly two elements. The number of vertices of degree zero in G is: A. 1  B. n  C. n+1  D. 2^n
questions.to_find: How many vertices (subsets) have no neighbour at all.

understand.plain: Each vertex is a subset of an n-element set. Two subsets are joined only when they share exactly 2 elements. A vertex has degree zero when NO other subset can meet it in exactly two elements. A subset can never reach a 2-element overlap if it has fewer than 2 elements of its own. So count the subsets of size 0 and size 1.

keywords.explain: a vertex with no edges - nothing is adjacent to it
keywords.example: the empty set shares 0 elements with everything, so it touches nobody

keywords.explain: the two subsets must have |A intersect B| = 2, not more and not less
subsets of an n-set grouped by how many elements they hold
keywords.example: 1 empty set, n singletons, C(n,2) pairs, ...

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Isolated subsets by size'><title>Isolated subsets by size</title><desc>Subsets of size 0 and size 1 can never overlap another subset in exactly two elements, so all n+1 of them are isolated.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='60' width='160' height='110' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>size 0</text><text x='120' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>1</text><text x='120' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>empty set</text><rect x='220' y='60' width='160' height='110' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>size 1</text><text x='300' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>n</text><text x='300' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>singletons</text><rect x='400' y='60' width='160' height='110' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='480' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>size >= 2</text><text x='480' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>has neighbours</text><text x='480' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>degree > 0</text><text x='300' y='212' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>isolated = 1 + n = n + 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The empty set and the n single-element subsets are isolated; every subset of size 2 or more has at least one neighbour, giving n+1 degree-zero vertices.

given.aim: Count the vertices of G that have degree zero.

terms.meaning: size of the ground set, n >= 6
terms.example: if n = 6 the ground set is {1,...,6}

terms.meaning: a vertex of G = one subset of the ground set
terms.connects: its size decides whether it can ever overlap another set in 2 elements
needs A to hold at least 2 elements

terms.example: {3} can share at most 1 element, so never exactly 2
terms.connects: exactly the subsets of size 0 and 1

given.plan: A subset can hit |intersection| = 2 only if it owns at least 2 elements. So the isolated vertices are precisely the size-0 and size-1 subsets; count those two groups and add.

steps.title: Which subsets can never overlap in 2 elements
steps.apply: if |A| <= 1 then |A intersect B| <= 1 < 2 for every B, so A is isolated
steps.note: you cannot share 2 elements when you own fewer than 2

steps.title: Count the isolated subsets
steps.note: one empty set plus n singletons; every size-2 subset like {x,y} has a neighbour {x,y,z} since n >= 6

questions.formula_note: Key idea: |A intersect B| <= |A|, so only subsets of size 0 or 1 are forced to have degree zero; that is 1 + n = n+1.

questions.question: The 2^n vertices of a graph G corresponds to all subsets of a set of size n, for n >= 6. Two vertices of G are adjacent if and only if the corresponding sets intersect in exactly two elements. The maximum degree of a vertex in G is: A. C(n/2, 2) * 2^(n/2)  B. 2^(n-2)  C. 2^(n-3) x 3  D. 2^(n-1)
questions.to_find: The largest possible degree of any vertex (subset) in G.

understand.plain: Pick a subset A of size k. Its neighbours are the subsets B with |A intersect B| = 2: choose which 2 of A's elements are shared, then freely build the rest of B from the n-k elements OUTSIDE A (B must touch no other element of A). That gives C(k,2) * 2^(n-k) neighbours. Now find the k that maximises this.

keywords.explain: how many other subsets meet it in exactly two elements
keywords.example: a 3-element set A shares 2 elements with many supersets/partial sets

keywords.explain: elements not in A can be in or out of B with no restriction
keywords.example: each of the n-k outside elements gives a factor of 2

keywords.explain: try each subset size and keep the size giving the most neighbours
keywords.example: k = 3 and k = 4 both peak the count

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Degree as a function of subset size'><title>Degree as a function of subset size</title><desc>Neighbour count C(k,2)*2^(n-k) rises to a peak at k=3 and k=4, where it equals 3*2^(n-3).</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='40' width='110' height='90' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='85' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>k = 2</text><text x='85' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2^(n-2)</text><rect x='160' y='40' width='130' height='90' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='225' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>k = 3</text><text x='225' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3*2^(n-3)</text><rect x='310' y='40' width='130' height='90' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='375' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>k = 4</text><text x='375' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>6*2^(n-4)</text><rect x='460' y='40' width='130' height='90' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='525' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>k = 5</text><text x='525' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>10*2^(n-5)</text><text x='310' y='178' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>degree(A) = C(k,2) * 2^(n-k),  k = |A|</text><text x='310' y='216' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>peak at k = 3 or 4  ->  3 * 2^(n-3)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Bar comparison of neighbour counts for subset sizes 2,3,4,5; the maximum 3*2^(n-3) is reached at sizes 3 and 4.

given.aim: Maximise the number of subsets meeting a chosen subset A in exactly two elements.

terms.meaning: the chosen subset and its size k = |A|
terms.connects: we optimise the degree over the choice of k

terms.meaning: the 2 elements of A that must lie in B
terms.connects: C(k,2) ways to fix the overlap

terms.meaning: the n-k elements not in A, free to be in or out of B

given.plan: Count neighbours of a size-k subset as C(k,2)*2^(n-k) using the Product Rule, then compare consecutive sizes to find the maximising k.

steps.title: Neighbour count of a size-k subset
steps.apply: pick 2 shared elements from A in C(k,2) ways; the n-k outside elements are free -> 2^(n-k); no other element of A may appear in B
steps.note: B must intersect A in EXACTLY the chosen 2 elements, so the remaining k-2 elements of A are forced out

steps.title: Maximise over k
steps.note: f(k)/f(k-1) = k / (2(k-2)) >= 1 only up to k = 4, so the peak is at k = 3 and k = 4

questions.formula_note: The degree of a size-k subset is C(k,2)*2^(n-k); comparing successive k shows it peaks at k=3,4 giving the maximum 3*2^(n-3).

questions.question: Which one of the following is TRUE for any simple connected undirected graph with more than 2 vertices? A. No two vertices have the same degree.  B. At least two vertices have the same degree.  C. At least three vertices have the same degree.  D. All vertices have the same degree.
questions.answer: B. At least two vertices have the same degree.
questions.to_find: Which degree statement must always hold for such a graph.

understand.plain: In a simple graph on n vertices every degree lies between 0 and n-1, giving n possible values. But degree 0 (isolated) and degree n-1 (joined to all) cannot both appear at once. So really only n-1 distinct degrees are usable for n vertices - by the Pigeonhole Principle two vertices must share a degree.

keywords.explain: no loops and no repeated edges, so a vertex degree is between 0 and n-1
keywords.example: on 4 vertices a degree can be 0,1,2 or 3

keywords.explain: n items into fewer than n boxes forces two items to share a box
keywords.example: n degrees into n-1 possible values -> a repeat

keywords.explain: if one vertex touches everyone, no vertex can be isolated
keywords.example: in K-style a degree-(n-1) vertex rules out a degree-0 vertex

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Pigeonhole on degrees'><title>Pigeonhole on degrees</title><desc>n vertices but only n-1 attainable degree values forces at least two vertices to share a degree.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='300' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>n vertices  ->  only n-1 usable degrees</text><rect x='40' y='70' width='150' height='70' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='115' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>n vertices</text><text x='240' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='big mut'>-></text><rect x='300' y='70' width='150' height='70' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='375' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>degrees in</text><text x='375' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>{0..n-1}</text><text x='300' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>but 0 and n-1 cannot both occur</text><text x='300' y='224' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>=> two vertices share a degree</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: n vertices map into only n-1 possible degree values, so by pigeonhole at least two vertices have equal degree.

given.aim: Decide which degree property is guaranteed for every simple connected graph with n > 2.

terms.meaning: an isolated vertex and a universal vertex cannot coexist
terms.example: if some vertex has degree n-1, no degree 0 exists
terms.connects: removes one slot, leaving only n-1 usable

given.plan: Show the n vertices can only take n-1 different degree values, then apply the Pigeonhole Principle to force a repeated degree.

steps.title: Count usable degree values
steps.formula_raw: possible degrees subset {0,1,...,n-1}, but not both 0 and n-1
steps.apply: degrees 0 and n-1 are mutually exclusive, so at most n-1 distinct degree values can actually occur
steps.note: either no isolated vertex, or no universal vertex - never both

steps.title: Apply the Pigeonhole Principle
steps.formula_raw: n objects into <= n-1 boxes => some box has >= 2
steps.apply: n vertices placed into at most n-1 degree values => two vertices land on the same degree
steps.note: this guarantees option B; A, C and D fail on simple examples like a path P3

solution.result: At least two vertices have the same degree  (Option B)

questions.formula_note: Pigeonhole on degrees: n vertices but only n-1 attainable degree values (since 0 and n-1 cannot co-occur) force at least two equal degrees.

questions.question: Let G = (V, E) be a graph. Define xi(G) = sum_d (i_d * d), where i_d is the number of vertices of degree d in G. If S and T are two different trees with xi(S) = xi(T), then A. |S| = 2|T|  B. |S| = |T| - 1  C. |S| = |T|  D. |S| = |T| + 1
questions.to_find: The relation between the vertex counts |S| and |T| when xi(S) = xi(T).

understand.plain: The quantity xi(G) just adds up every vertex's degree: a vertex of degree d is counted d times, so xi(G) = sum of all degrees = 2|E| (Handshaking Lemma). A tree on |V| vertices always has exactly |V|-1 edges, so xi = 2(|V|-1). Equal xi therefore forces equal vertex counts.

keywords.explain: i_d*d counts each degree-d vertex d times, i.e. it is the degree sum
keywords.example: if 3 vertices have degree 2, they contribute 3*2 = 6

keywords.explain: the sum of all vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges
a tree on V vertices has exactly V-1 edges
keywords.example: a path on 5 vertices has 4 edges

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='xi equals twice the edge count'><title>xi equals twice the edge count</title><desc>xi(G) is the degree sum = 2|E|; for a tree |E| = |V|-1, so equal xi means equal vertex counts.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='60' width='150' height='80' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='115' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>xi(G)</text><text x='115' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>= 2|E|</text><text x='225' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='big mut'>=</text><rect x='260' y='60' width='170' height='80' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='345' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>tree: |E| = |V|-1</text><text x='345' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>= 2(|V|-1)</text><text x='300' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>xi(S) = xi(T)  =>  2(|S|-1) = 2(|T|-1)</text><text x='300' y='224' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>=>  |S| = |T|</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: xi(G) equals the degree sum 2|E|; a tree has |E| = |V|-1, so equal xi values force equal vertex counts |S| = |T|.

given.aim: Relate |S| and |T| given that two trees have the same xi value.

terms.meaning: number of vertices of degree d
terms.example: in a path of 5 nodes, i_1 = 2, i_2 = 3

terms.meaning: weighted degree count = sum_d i_d * d
the number of vertices in trees S and T

terms.connects: turns xi into a function of |V| alone

given.plan: Recognise xi as the degree sum 2|E|, substitute the tree relation |E| = |V|-1, then set xi(S) = xi(T) and solve.

steps.title: xi is just the degree sum
steps.formula_raw: xi(G) = sum_d i_d * d = sum_v deg(v) = 2|E|
steps.apply: counting each vertex deg(v) times is the same as adding all degrees, which is 2|E|
steps.note: this is exactly the Handshaking Lemma rewritten by degree class

steps.title: Use the tree edge count and equate
steps.note: any two trees with equal degree sum have equal vertex counts, regardless of shape

questions.formula_note: xi(G) is the degree sum = 2|E| (Handshaking); for a tree |E| = |V|-1, so equal xi directly gives |S| = |T|.

questions.question: The degree sequence of a simple graph is the sequence of the degrees of the nodes in the graph in decreasing order. Which of the following sequences can not be the degree sequence of any graph? I. 7,6,5,4,4,3,2,1  II. 6,6,6,6,3,3,2,2  III. 7,6,6,4,4,3,2,2  IV. 8,7,7,6,4,2,1,1  A. I and II  B. III and IV  C. IV only  D. II and IV
questions.to_find: Which of the four sequences are NOT graphical (cannot be any simple graph's degrees).

understand.plain: Each sequence has 8 terms, so the graph would have 8 vertices and every degree must be at most 7. Sequence IV starts with 8, which is impossible for 8 vertices. For the rest we run the Havel-Hakimi test: repeatedly remove the top degree d and subtract 1 from the next d entries; if we ever go negative the sequence is not graphical. Doing this rules out II as well.

keywords.explain: the list of vertex degrees written in non-increasing order
a sequence that really is the degree list of some simple graph
remove the largest degree d, subtract 1 from the next d entries, repeat; a negative value means not graphical

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Which sequences are graphical'><title>Which sequences are graphical</title><desc>Sequence IV fails because degree 8 exceeds n-1=7; sequence II fails the Havel-Hakimi reduction; I and III pass.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='40' width='270' height='44' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='48' y='68' class='lbl ok'>I.</text><text x='300' y='68' text-anchor='end' class='lbl ok'>7,6,5,4,4,3,2,1  OK</text><rect x='320' y='40' width='270' height='44' rx='8' class='no' fill-opacity='0.18'/><text x='338' y='68' class='lbl no'>II.</text><text x='585' y='68' text-anchor='end' class='lbl no'>6,6,6,6,3,3,2,2  X</text><rect x='30' y='96' width='270' height='44' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='48' y='124' class='lbl ok'>III.</text><text x='300' y='124' text-anchor='end' class='lbl ok'>7,6,6,4,4,3,2,2  OK</text><rect x='320' y='96' width='270' height='44' rx='8' class='no' fill-opacity='0.18'/><text x='338' y='124' class='lbl no'>IV.</text><text x='585' y='124' text-anchor='end' class='lbl no'>8,7,7,6,4,2,1,1  X</text><text x='310' y='184' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>IV: degree 8 > n-1 = 7 (impossible)</text><text x='310' y='214' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>II: Havel-Hakimi goes negative</text><text x='310' y='244' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>not graphical: II and IV</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Of four 8-term sequences, IV has an impossible degree 8 and II fails the Havel-Hakimi reduction, so II and IV are not graphical while I and III are.

given.aim: Identify which listed degree sequences cannot be realised by any simple graph.

terms.meaning: number of vertices = length of each sequence
the total of all degrees must be even (= 2|E|)
no degree may exceed n-1 in a simple graph
iteratively peel the top degree to test realisability
terms.example: if a value turns negative, reject
terms.connects: decides the borderline cases II and III

given.plan: First rule out any sequence with a degree > n-1 (kills IV), then apply the Havel-Hakimi test to the remaining sequences to see which reduce to all zeros.

steps.title: Max-degree check (n = 8)
steps.apply: IV begins 8,... but max allowed degree is n-1 = 7, so IV is impossible immediately
steps.note: a vertex in a simple graph on 8 nodes can join at most 7 others

steps.title: Havel-Hakimi on II: 6,6,6,6,3,3,2,2
steps.formula_raw: remove top d, subtract 1 from next d, re-sort, repeat
steps.apply: 6:6,6,6,3,3,2,2->5,5,5,2,2,1,1; 5:5,5,2,2,1,1->4,4,1,1,0,1=4,4,1,1,1,0; 4:4,1,1,1,0->3,0,0,0,0; remove 3 needs three positive entries but only zeros remain -> negative -> NOT graphical
steps.note: II fails, even though its sum 34 is even

steps.title: Havel-Hakimi on I and III (both pass)
steps.apply: I: 7,6,5,4,4,3,2,1 reduces cleanly to all zeros; III: 7,6,6,4,4,3,2,2 reduces cleanly to all zeros -> both graphical
steps.note: so only II and IV are non-graphical

solution.result: II and IV are not graphical  (Option D)

questions.formula_note: IV dies on the max-degree rule (8 > n-1 = 7); II dies under Havel-Hakimi; I and III reduce to all zeros, so the non-graphical pair is II and IV.

formulas.name: Edges in a Tree
formulas.plain: a tree on |V| vertices has exactly |V|-1 edges (and is acyclic and connected)
formulas.whenToUse: whenever a graph is stated to be a tree (or forest) and you need to convert between vertex count, edge count, or degree sum

terms.means: number of vertices in the tree
number of edges in the tree

formulas.trap: this holds for a single tree; a forest with c components has |E| = |V| - c, so do not apply |V|-1 to a disconnected forest
formulas.reference: Tree characterisation (connected + acyclic) in standard graph theory

formulas.name: Havel-Hakimi Graphical Sequence Test
formulas.latex: d_1 \ge d_2 \ge \cdots \ge d_n \text{ graphical} \iff (d_2-1,\, d_3-1,\, \ldots,\, d_{d_1+1}-1,\, d_{d_1+2},\, \ldots,\, d_n) \text{ graphical}
formulas.plain: to test if a non-increasing degree sequence is realisable by a simple graph: remove the largest degree d, subtract 1 from the next d entries, re-sort, and repeat; the sequence is graphical iff this reduces to all zeros without producing a negative value
formulas.whenToUse: to decide whether a given list of integers can be the degree sequence of some simple graph

terms.means: the degree sequence in non-increasing order
number of vertices = length of the sequence

formulas.trap: necessary quick checks first: the degree sum must be even and every degree must be <= n-1; passing these does NOT prove graphical, but failing either instantly proves not graphical
formulas.reference: Havel (1955), Hakimi (1962); equivalent to the Erdos-Gallai conditions
